ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the bus architecture used by ARINC 429 — one transmitter broadcasting to multiple receivers on a dedicated wire pair.
choices: Multi-drop bidirectional
Token-passing ring
Point-to-multipoint
Peer-to-peer full-duplex
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a unidirectional, point-to-multipoint broadcast architecture: one transmitter, up to 20 receivers, no multi-drop with multiple talkers. Multi-drop bidirectional describes 1553 and RS-485. Token-passing describes token-ring networks, not ARINC 429.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the maximum data rate defined by the ARINC 429 standard.
choices: 12 kbps
1 Mbps
10 Mbps
100 kbps
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429's high-speed rate is 100 kilobits per second — the maximum defined by the standard. 12 kbps is the low-speed rate. 1 Mbps is the data rate of MIL-STD-1553. 10 Mbps is well above any ARINC 429 rate.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the primary purpose of the ARINC 429 databus in modern avionics.
choices: To transfer standardized digital data between avionics units such as air data
To provide primary flight control inputs to the autopilot servos
To supply electrical power to remote sensors
To connect the engine FADEC to fuel metering valves
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 is a data-transfer standard for avionics inter-unit communication — sharing values such as altitude, airspeed, position, and status between boxes. It does not carry power, flight control commands, or engine control signals.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the most widely deployed digital databus standard across commercial and general-aviation avionics.
choices: MIL-STD-1553
CAN bus
IEEE 1394
ARINC 429
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 is the dominant digital databus in civilian aviation. MIL-STD-1553 is widely used in military applications. CAN bus is common in automotive and some UAV systems. IEEE 1394 (FireWire) is seldom used in aviation.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This 8-bit field at the start of every ARINC 429 word tells a receiver which parameter — altitude, airspeed, heading, etc. — the word contains.
choices: Label
Preamble
Destination tag
Parity block
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: The Label is the first 8 bits of every 32-bit ARINC 429 word. It is octal-coded (for example, label 203 = altitude, 206 = computed airspeed). Receivers filter the transmitter's stream by Label and accept only the parameters they need. ARINC 429 does not use a separate preamble or destination tag, and parity is a single bit at the end of the word, not a block.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the protocol model used by MIL-STD-1553, in which a single controller initiates every transaction.
choices: Command/response with a bus controller
Peer-to-peer with collision detection
Token-passing ring
Broadcast-only with no acknowledgements
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 uses a command/response model with a single bus controller directing all traffic. Peer-to-peer with collision detection describes Ethernet. Token ring is a different LAN architecture. Broadcast-only with no acknowledgements better describes ARINC 429.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the function of the bus controller in a MIL-STD-1553 system.
choices: To initiate all bus transactions, schedule messages, and direct data
To passively monitor bus traffic without issuing commands
To provide power to the remote terminals
To convert analog signals from sensors into digital words
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: The bus controller initiates every transaction on the bus — issuing commands, scheduling messages, and routing data. Passive monitoring describes a bus monitor (BM), a separate role. The BC does not supply power or perform A/D conversion.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: When a replacement RT is not detected on a MIL-STD-1553 bus, this is the first item to verify.
choices: The aircraft weather radar settings
The RT's address strapping and coupler/termination wiring
The flight control computer's firmware version
The aircraft's GPS almanac
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: RT address strapping and physical bus connection (coupler, termination, stub length) are the top causes of a 1553 device not being detected. Weather radar settings, FCC firmware, and GPS almanac are unrelated.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the main architectural difference between ARINC 429 and MIL-STD-1553.
choices: ARINC 429 is faster than MIL-STD-1553
ARINC 429 is unidirectional broadcast with no controller
ARINC 429 is used only in helicopters; MIL-STD-1553 is used only in jets
ARINC 429 uses optical fiber; MIL-STD-1553 uses copper
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: The architectural distinction is topology and protocol: 429 is unidirectional broadcast (no controller), 1553 is bidirectional multi-drop with a dedicated bus controller. MIL-STD-1553 is faster (1 Mbps vs 100 kbps), not slower. Both standards use copper twisted-shielded pairs in normal installations.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the primary reason MIL-STD-1553 wires every Remote Terminal to two parallel cables (Bus A and Bus B).
choices: To double the data rate for high-bandwidth applications
Redundancy — traffic continues on the other bus if one fails
To separate command traffic from response traffic
To let two Bus Controllers share the bus workload
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 was designed for flight-critical military applications where a single wire fault must not disable the system. The two buses carry identical traffic; the Bus Controller normally uses Bus A, and if it sees errors it retries on Bus B. The data rate is 1 Mbps on either bus — using both does NOT double it. And 1553 allows only one active Bus Controller at a time.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: On an ARINC 429 bus, the 'HI' differential voltage that represents a binary '1' sits near this level.
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: ARINC 429 uses approximately +10 V differential for HI (binary 1), −10 V differential for LO (binary 0), and 0 V for the null between bits. The +3.3 V and +5 V levels are common in logic-family signaling (CMOS/TTL), not ARINC 429. +24 V is typical of power distribution, not databus signaling.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is a key characteristic of RS-232 signals.
choices: Differential signaling at ±10 V
Single-ended signaling referenced to ground at
Current-loop signaling at 20 mA
Optical pulses at infrared wavelengths
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-232 is single-ended: each signal is referenced to a common ground, with voltages typically in the ±3 V to ±15 V range. Differential signaling at ±10 V describes ARINC 429. Current-loop at 20 mA is a different legacy signaling scheme. RS-232 is electrical, not optical.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is the typical wiring configuration used by RS-485 in aircraft systems.
choices: A differential twisted pair shared by up to 32 transmitters and receivers in a multi-drop bus
A single-ended wire referenced to ground
A fiber-optic ring with regenerative repeaters
Three separate wires for transmit, receive, and ground with no termination
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-485 uses a differential twisted pair on a multi-drop bus supporting up to 32 drivers and 32 receivers. Single-ended wiring is RS-232. RS-485 is electrical, not optical. Termination resistors at each end of the bus are standard to prevent reflections.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is the key difference between RS-422 and RS-485.
choices: RS-422 uses optical fiber while RS-485 uses copper
RS-422 supports one driver and up to 10 receivers
RS-422 is single-ended while RS-485 is differential
RS-422 runs at 10 Mbps while RS-485 is limited to 100 bps
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-422 is one-talker / many-listener; RS-485 allows many talkers and listeners to share the same bus. Both are differential (neither is single-ended). Both use copper. Both support a range of data rates — the distinction is in driver count, not peak rate.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This limitation of RS-232 makes it impossible to simply tap a third device onto an existing GPS-to-FMS serial link.
choices: The need for a bus controller that RS-232 does not include
A required 120-ohm terminator that most RS-232 devices lack
RS-232 is point-to-point, supporting only two devices per link
Insufficient data rate to carry GPS data at the aircraft's update rate
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-232 is strictly point-to-point — one link connects exactly two devices. Tapping a third device on the same pair does not work. To add a third you need a second RS-232 link, a multi-drop standard like RS-485, or a gateway that fans out. RS-232 has no bus controller, no 120-ohm terminator, and more than enough speed for GPS data.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is a key advantage of digital databuses like ARINC 429 over traditional analog signal wiring.
choices: Ability to multiplex many data values on one wire pair with built-in error
Higher voltages that are less susceptible to lightning strikes
Elimination of the need for receivers in avionics units
Reduced need for any shielding on cable runs
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Multiplexing (many data values per wire pair) and built-in error checking (parity bit) are core advantages. Digital signals are NOT higher voltage than typical aircraft levels, receivers are still required, and shielding remains important to minimize EMI.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is the number system essential to digital avionics data processing.
choices: Decimal (base 10)
Binary (base 2)
Octal (base 8)
Hexadecimal (base 16)
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: All digital electronics ultimately store and process binary (base-2) data — each bit is a 0 or 1. Octal and hexadecimal are convenient human shorthand (base 8 and base 16 respectively) that map to groups of bits, but the underlying processing is always binary.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: In the context of avionics data buses, this is what 'word length' refers to.
choices: The physical length of the cable required for one bit
The time in milliseconds that one word takes to transmit
The number of words a bus can carry per second
The total number of bits that make up a single transmitted word
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Word length is the total bit count of a single word — 32 bits for ARINC 429, 20 bits for MIL-STD-1553. Cable length, transmission time, and throughput are related but distinct concepts.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: In avionics, this acronym stands for 'Built-In Test Equipment' — a self-monitoring feature that reports a unit's health.
choices: BITS
BIST
BITE
BIT
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: BITE stands for Built-In Test Equipment. BITS is not a standard avionics acronym. BIST (Built-In Self-Test) is a related semiconductor-design term. BIT (Built-In Test) is the test itself; BITE is the equipment that performs the test.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is what the parity bit in an ARINC 429 or MIL-STD-1553 word lets the receiver do.
choices: Synchronize the receiver's clock to the transmitter's bit rate
Identify which transmitter on the bus originated the word
Measure the data rate the transmitter is currently using
Detect whether a bit was corrupted during transmission
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Parity is a single-bit error-detection technique. ARINC 429 uses ODD parity — the parity bit is set so the total number of '1' bits in the 32-bit word is odd. If the receiver counts an EVEN number of 1's, at least one bit flipped and the word is dropped. Parity does NOT synchronize clocks (Manchester encoding does that for 1553), does NOT identify sources (the Label/RT address does), and does NOT measure rates.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is a common cause of intermittent communication loss on an ARINC 429 bus.
choices: The receiving unit is powered off
The transmitter is configured for the wrong data rate
A lightning strike in the previous flight
Chafed cable, loose pins, or a broken shield ground
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: Physical wiring issues — chafing, loose pins, broken shield grounds — are the top causes of intermittent ARINC 429 loss. A powered-off receiver would cause continuous (not intermittent) loss, as would a data-rate mismatch. A lightning strike would typically produce persistent damage, not intermittent symptoms.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is the appropriate first step when ARINC 429 data errors are observed in a system test.
choices: Verify physical-layer integrity
Replace the transmitting unit
Reconfigure all receivers to a different data rate
Update the aircraft flight manual
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: Physical-layer faults cause most bus errors. Always start with cable continuity, connectors, and shield grounding. Replacing units or reconfiguring receivers without evidence of their involvement wastes time and can introduce new problems.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is the primary purpose of a digital databus analyzer in avionics troubleshooting.
choices: To supply electrical power to the bus during testing
To physically repair damaged wiring
To calibrate the aircraft's altimeter
To capture and decode bus traffic so technicians can verify data
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: A databus analyzer captures traffic and decodes it into readable words (labels, data fields, parity status, etc.). It does not supply power, perform physical repairs, or calibrate analog instruments — those are different tools.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is a primary purpose of using a digital databus analyzer in a general-aviation avionics shop.
choices: To clean oxidation from connector pins
To physically re-terminate cables
To verify and troubleshoot data exchange between integrated
To perform altimeter static-pressure leak checks
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: A GA shop uses the databus analyzer to verify correct data exchange between integrated avionics and to isolate intermittent problems that cannot be resolved with a multimeter or oscilloscope alone. The other options describe unrelated maintenance tasks.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: A 1553 bus that worked for months begins showing intermittent bit errors after an avionics-bay inspection. A bus analyzer shows 'ringing' on the waveform. This is the most likely cause.
choices: A 78-ohm terminator has come loose or open-circuited
The Bus Controller software version is out of date
The Remote Terminals are drawing excessive bus current
The 1 Mbps data rate has drifted outside spec
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 requires a 78-ohm terminator at each end of the main cable. A missing or damaged terminator causes the signal to reflect off the end and collide with new transmissions — exactly the 'ringing' seen on an analyzer. Onset right after maintenance points to a terminator that was disturbed. BC software, RT current draw, and data-rate drift produce different symptoms, not ringing.

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This is the type of bus architecture employed in an avionics system that uses ARINC 429.
choices: Shared multi-master with collision detection
One-way broadcast, one transmitter and up to 20 receivers
Token-passing ring among paired terminals
Full-duplex peer-to-peer mesh
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a one-way broadcast topology: a single transmitter drives a dedicated twisted shielded pair, and up to 20 receivers listen. Multi-master, token ring, and peer-to-peer mesh describe other networking standards.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: In an avionics system using ARINC 429, what type of bus architecture is employed?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: On the ARINC 429 bus, a logic-1 is signaled at roughly this differential level.
choices: About +3 V
About +5 V
About +28 V
About +10 V
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: ARINC 429 represents a binary one by a differential pulse of about +10 V across the twisted pair. Plus 3 and plus 5 V are common logic-family levels, and plus 28 V is an aircraft DC bus, not a databus level.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: In ARINC 429, what is the typical voltage used to represent a binary "I"?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: A MIL-STD-1553 databus shows non-idle activity longer than expected. This is a possible cause.
choices: Scheduled idle time within a normal major frame
Bus controller retries or a damaged 78 Ω terminator
An expired flight manual supplement on the aircraft
Cockpit night-mode lighting engaged
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Extended non-idle activity points to retries driven by bad error responses from a remote terminal, a stuck transmitter, or reflections from a bad 78 ohm terminator. Scheduled idle time looks idle, not busy, and unrelated paperwork or lighting does not affect bus state.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are analyzing a MIL-STD-1553 databus and find that the bus is operating in a non-idle state for longer than expected. What could be a possible cause?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: A flight management computer interface specification mentions a BITE message. This is what BITE stands for.
choices: Bus Interface Test Element
Built-In Test Equipment
Binary Input Transfer Equipment
Broadcast Integrated Terminal Encoder
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: In avionics, BITE stands for Built-In Test Equipment, the self-monitoring feature inside an LRU that reports diagnostic status. The other expansions are not standard acronyms in avionics documentation.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: A digital interface specification for a flight management computer mentions “Bite Message”. What is “BITE”?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: Among the following, this statement is the one that correctly characterizes the MIL-STD-1553 bus.
choices: Peer-to-peer transmission by any unit whenever the bus is quiet
One-way broadcast from one transmitter to up to 20 receivers
Command/response run by a single BC with up to 31 RTs
Continuous token passing between matched pairs of remote terminals
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 is a command/response protocol: a single bus controller initiates every transaction and up to 31 remote terminals respond only when addressed. Peer-to-peer, one-way broadcast, and token passing describe other standards.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Which of the following best describes the MIL-STD-1553 data transmission protocol?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: With an ARINC-fed digital autopilot on the bench, this preparation step belongs on the checklist first.
choices: Verify ARINC wiring, shield grounding, and software configuration against approved installation data
Remove all ARINC interconnect pins so only the internal gyros drive the servos
Power up the autopilot and cycle every servo before checking the installation
Skip the manufacturer procedure because self-test covers the whole check
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Before a functional check, verify the ARINC wiring, shield grounds, and the autopilot software configuration against approved installation data, then follow the manufacturer procedure. Pre-cycling servos or skipping the procedure creates safety and airworthiness issues.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Before a functional check of a digital autopilot using ARINC data, you should:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: When a digital databus system is failing intermittently, this test instrument helps the most.
choices: A mechanical torque wrench calibrated for connector backshells
A panel-mount analog voltmeter reading DC offset
A static-pressure leak tester connected to the pitot system
A digital bus analyzer that captures ARINC 429 or 1553 traffic
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Intermittent digital databus faults call for a bus analyzer, which captures live traffic and decodes parity, framing, and missing labels as they occur. Torque wrenches, analog voltmeters, and leak testers address unrelated tasks.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: When troubleshooting a digital databus system failing intermittently, which test instrument helps most?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This best describes ARINC 429 in a single phrase.
choices: One-way bus, one transmitter, up to 20 receivers on twisted pair
A 28 volt DC power feeder that supplies avionics equipment
A mechanical cable system that drives the control surfaces
A pneumatic reference line that supplies the air data computer
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: ARINC 429 is best described as a one-way digital data bus with a single transmitter, up to 20 receivers, and a dedicated twisted shielded pair. It is not pneumatic, not a power bus, and not a mechanical control run.

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the typical wiring configuration used in an RS-485 databus system.
choices: Two single-ended wires referenced to chassis ground and left unshielded
A single coaxial cable with 75 ohm BNC connectors
A shielded differential twisted pair shared by up to 32 devices, terminated at each end
A multimode optical fiber looped through every device
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: RS-485 uses a shielded differential twisted pair supporting up to 32 devices on a multi-drop bus with termination resistors (typically 120 ohms) at each end. Single-ended wiring describes RS-232, and coax and fiber are not used by RS-485.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: In an RS-485 databus system, what type of wiring configuration is typically used?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: Among RS-232 databus traits, this one shows up commonly on the spec sheet.
choices: Transformer-coupled Manchester encoding at 1 megabit per second on a twisted shielded pair
Differential signaling at plus or minus 10 volts with up to 20 receivers per pair, broadcast only
Single-ended signaling referenced to ground at plus or minus 3 to 15 volts, point-to-point
Optical pulses transmitted over a plastic multimode fiber between two avionics units
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: RS-232 is single-ended, referenced to ground, at about plus or minus 3 to 15 V, and is point-to-point between exactly two devices. Plus or minus 10 V differential is ARINC 429, Manchester at 1 Mbps is MIL-STD-1553, and RS-232 is not optical.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Which of the following is a common characteristic of the RS-232 digital databus standard?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: One device on a MIL-STD-1553 bus is not responding to commands from the bus controller. This is a likely cause.
choices: The aircraft weather radar is set to standby mode
Incorrect RT address strap, open stub, or missing power
An expired VOR receiver accuracy check is overdue
Cabin altitude control is left in manual mode
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: A single non-responsive remote terminal usually has a bad address strap, an open stub coupler, or a power/ground problem local to that terminal. Weather radar mode, cabin altitude, and VOR checks are unrelated.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are troubleshooting a system with multiple avionics components connected via MIL-STD-1553. You observe that one of the devices is not responding to commands from the bus controller. What is a likely cause of this issue?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: Among the claims about ARINC 429 data rates, this one is accurate.
choices: Both speeds run simultaneously on the same pair for redundancy
Transmitter and receiver auto-negotiate the bit rate on each word
ARINC 429 supports speeds up to 10 megabits per second
The standard defines two fixed speeds, about 12 kbps low and 100 kbps high
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: ARINC 429 defines two fixed rates: about 12 kbps low speed and 100 kbps high speed. Neither is negotiated, the peak is well under 10 Mbps, and a given pair carries only one of the two speeds at a time.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Which statement about ARINC 429 transmission speed is correct?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: A flight control computer is pushing bad attitude onto the bus because of bus corruption — this action must come first.
choices: Disengage the affected flight control function and isolate the aircraft to a safe configuration
Return the aircraft to service and schedule investigation at the next inspection
Replace every avionics box on the bus before further testing
Raise the autopilot authority so it can override the bad signal
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: Bad flight control data is flight-critical, so the first action is to disengage the affected function and safe the aircraft before investigating. Returning to service with known bad data, shotgun replacement, or boosting autopilot authority are all inappropriate.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: If a digital flight control computer sends erroneous attitude info due to databus corruption, first step?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: A technician sees high latency between data updates on an ARINC 429 bus. This is the most likely explanation.
choices: MIL-STD-1553 bus controller retries redirected onto the ARINC pair
Multi-master arbitration delays among competing transmitters on the pair
Slow transmit repetition, low-speed mode, or a receiver overloaded with labels
Per-word handshaking acknowledgments between transmitter and receiver nodes
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: ARINC 429 has one transmitter per pair and no arbitration or handshake. Latency grows with slow transmitter repetition, low-speed operation, or an overloaded receiver. The other options describe other standards or mechanisms that ARINC 429 does not use.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Scenario: In a complex avionics system, you notice high latency between data updates on an ARINC 429 bus. What is the most likely explanation?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: A replaced device on a MIL-STD-1553 bus is not detected after installation. This is the first item to verify.
choices: The aircraft GPS almanac expiration date
The cockpit voice recorder channel assignments
The RT address, stub coupler, and bus termination
The baggage door proximity switch alignment
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: A new or replaced 1553 device that is not detected most often has an incorrect RT address strap, a disconnected stub coupler, or missing bus termination. Unrelated avionics such as GPS almanac, voice recorder channels, or door switches are not relevant.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Scenario: You are replacing a failed device on a MIL-STD-1553 bus. After the replacement, the system does not detect the new device. What should be checked first?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: In every avionics databus, this element is the most basic building block of the digital system behind it.
choices: The bit, a single binary digit valued zero or one
A synchro resolver shaft angle
A pneumatic pressure pulse
An analog voltage referenced to a common ground
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: A bit, a single binary digit with value zero or one, is the fundamental building block of every digital system. Analog voltages, synchro shaft angles, and pneumatic pulses are not digital building blocks.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: What is the fundamental building block of digital systems?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: Data errors appear during a test of an ARINC 429 system. This is the first troubleshooting step.
choices: Replace the transmitting line replaceable unit on the spot
Verify physical layer: cable, connectors, and shield grounding
Reconfigure every receiver on the bus to a new bit rate
Perform a software revision update on every connected unit
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: The first step for ARINC 429 data errors is a physical-layer check of cable continuity, connector security, and shield grounding — these cause most errors. Shotgun replacement, rate changes, and blanket software updates are poor starting points.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Scenario: During an avionics system test, you notice data errors in the ARINC 429 communication. What is the FIRST step to troubleshoot the issue?

Extended Coverage 3.clue: For RS-422 or RS-485 links on light aircraft, this is the accepted wiring recipe.
choices: A single-ended unshielded wire referenced to chassis ground
A plastic optical fiber loop passed through each device
A 75 ohm coaxial cable with BNC taps at each device
A shielded differential twisted pair with termination resistors at each end
Extended Coverage 3.explanation: RS-422 and RS-485 use a shielded differential twisted pair with terminators at each end. Single-ended wiring is RS-232, and neither coax nor fiber is part of the RS-422 or RS-485 standard.
Extended Coverage 3.targetCaetStem: Which best describes a wiring practice for RS-422 or RS-485 in smaller aircraft systems?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: On an ARINC 429 or MIL-STD-1553 bus, this is the shop tool a technician reaches for to capture and decode traffic.
choices: A pitot-static test box used on the airspeed indicator
A mechanical torque wrench for connector backshells
An altimeter tester used for static-system certification
A digital bus analyzer that captures and decodes the databus words
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: A bus analyzer captures live ARINC 429 or 1553 traffic and decodes each word and error indication. Pitot-static, torque, and altimeter testers address unrelated tasks.
Extended Coverage 4.targetCaetStem: Which tool is commonly used to capture and analyze digital databus traffic?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: This is a common aircraft digital databus.
choices: ARINC 429
RG-58 coaxial cable
Synchro control transformer
Thermocouple reference junction
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: ARINC 429 is a common aircraft digital databus. RG-58 is a coaxial cable type, a synchro control transformer is an analog device, and a thermocouple reference junction is part of a temperature sensing circuit, not a databus.
Extended Coverage 4.targetCaetStem: Which of the following is a common aircraft digital databus?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: Intermittent loss of communication is reported between ARINC 429 devices. This is the first thing to check.
choices: The autopilot pitch trim servo current during steady-state cruise
The software revision of an unrelated LRU on a different bus
The aircraft fuel quantity indication at the time of the fault
Connector security, cable continuity, and shield grounding on the affected pair
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: Intermittent ARINC 429 loss is mostly a physical-layer issue, so the first check is the affected pair: connectors, continuity, and shield grounding. Autopilot current, fuel quantity, and unrelated software do not cause intermittent bus dropouts.
Extended Coverage 4.targetCaetStem: Scenario: An aircraft is using ARINC 429 for communication between its avionics components. You notice an intermittent loss of communication between devices. What should be the first thing to check?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: This is the main reason industry migrated modern avionics to digital buses such as ARINC 429.
choices: To exchange standardized digital data using relatively few wires
To serve as high-current DC power feeders for the servos
To provide structural grounding for the airframe
To physically support the weight of the avionics racks
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: Digital data buses like ARINC 429 exist to share standardized digital data between LRUs over a small number of wires, replacing heavy point-to-point analog wiring. They are not a grounding path, a power bus, or a mechanical support.
Extended Coverage 4.targetCaetStem: What is the main reason for using digital data buses like ARINC 429 in modern avionics?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: This statement best describes ARINC 429 as it is used in general-aviation avionics.
choices: A bidirectional command-and-response bus with a dedicated bus controller
A one-way broadcast bus, up to 20 receivers, 12 or 100 kbps
A multi-master shared bus at 1 megabit per second with dual-redundant cables
A point-to-point analog signal line referenced to chassis ground
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: In general-aviation avionics, ARINC 429 is best described as a one-way broadcast digital data bus: one transmitter, up to 20 receivers on a twisted shielded pair, running at 12 or 100 kbps. The other options describe MIL-STD-1553, other multi-master buses, or analog signal lines.
Extended Coverage 4.targetCaetStem: Which statement best describes ARINC429 used in GA avionics?

Extended Coverage 4.clue: Classified by architecture, ARINC 429 falls into this category of bus.
choices: One-way digital bus: one transmitter, up to 20 receivers
Mechanical control cable system for flight control surfaces
A pneumatic pitot-static reference line for air data units
A 28 volt DC power distribution bus for avionics equipment
Extended Coverage 4.explanation: ARINC 429 is primarily a one-way digital data bus with one transmitter and up to 20 receivers on a twisted shielded pair. It is not a power bus, a mechanical control system, or a pneumatic reference line.

final.clue: An ARINC 429 data word is exactly 32 bits long. Which bit acts as the parity bit for error checking?
choices: Bit 1
Bit 8
Bit 32
There is no parity bit
final.explanation: ARINC 429 words are 32 bits long. Bit 32 is exclusively used as an Odd Parity bit—set to 1 or 0 to ensure the total number of 1s in the word is an odd number, allowing basic error detection.

final.clue: MIL-STD-1553 uses a bi-directional, half-duplex architecture controlled by a Bus Controller. The devices responding to the controller are known as what?
choices: Slaves
Remote Terminal
Transceivers
Sub-systems
final.explanation: In a 1553 bus, the central controller is the Bus Controller (BC), and it commands up to 31 Remote Terminals (RTs) that act upon those commands.

final.clue: The AFDX (Avionics Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet) standard, used extensively on modern aircraft like the A380 and 787, is defined by which ARINC specification?
choices: ARINC 429
ARINC 717
ARINC 664
ARINC 818
final.explanation: ARINC 664 (specifically Part 7) defines AFDX, which brings deterministic Ethernet networking to avionics, vastly reducing wiring weight compared to point-to-point ARINC 429.
